This is consistent with Einstein's special theory of relativity, which holds that nothing travels faster than the speed of light [d2]. Some alternative or non-mainstream theories have proposed that gravity could be faster than light, but these are not accepted within the standard scientific framework [d1].

retrieved_docs.snippet: Whither Thou Goest "Whither Thou Goest" is a popular song written by Guy Singer. The song was published in 1954. The words are adapted from the Bible (Ruth 1:16-17) (King James Version). The most popular version was recorded by Les Paul and Mary Ford. Other versions were made by Perry Como (for his album "When You Come to the End of the Day"), Bing Crosby included the song in a medley on his album "On the Sentimental Side" (1962), George Morgan (see below), Mahalia Jackson (included in her album "Great Songs of Love and Faith" {1962}) and Leonard Cohen. The

Stand by Me (Charles Albert Tindley song) "Stand by Me" should not be confused with "Stand by Me Father", by Sam Cooke and James W. Alexander. Though sometimes known as "Stand by Me Father", "Stand by Me" is a 1905 gospel song by the Rev. Charles Albert Tindley. Although often thought to be a "traditional spiritual", Tindley copyrighted the song in 1905. The song of the same name by Ben E. King draws on Tindley's song. The song has been sung in Tindley's original gospel form by various artists including Bob Dylan. The song has been recorded by artists including:

Billy Steinberg William Endfield "Billy" Steinberg (born February 26, 1950) is an American songwriter. He achieved his greatest success in the 1980s with songwriting partner Tom Kelly; together the two wrote or co-wrote the No. 1 hits "Like a Virgin" by Madonna (1984), "True Colors" by Cyndi Lauper (1986), "Eternal Flame" by the Bangles (1989), "So Emotional" by Whitney Houston (1987) and "Alone" (covered by Heart in 1987). They also wrote or co-wrote the hit songs "I Drove All Night" (recorded by various artists, 1987), "I Touch Myself" by Divinyls (1990), and "I'll Stand By You" by The Pretenders (1994).

expected_response.answer: Mass and gravity are fundamentally linked: gravity is defined as an interaction between masses, meaning without mass, there is no gravity [d1]. This is reinforced by the basic principle that gravity pulls anything with mass together, which is why gravity—despite being weaker than electromagnetism—dominates the large-scale structure of the universe, since matter is overall charge-neutral [d3]. According to Newton's theory of gravity, the gravitational force on a particle with zero mass would be zero, which is why massless photons should not be affected by gravity under Newtonian mechanics; explaining why they are affected requires General Relativity [d4]. Additionally, the mathematical relationship between mass and gravity extends to how gravitational forces act on every particle in a body, with the total mass and its distribution determining the center of gravity [d5].

Osteoma An osteoma (plural: "osteomata") is a new piece of bone usually growing on another piece of bone, typically the skull. It is a benign tumor. When the bone tumor grows on other bone it is known as "homoplastic osteoma"; when it grows on other tissue it is called "heteroplastic osteoma". Osteoma represents the most common benign neoplasm of the nose and paranasal sinuses. The cause of osteomata is uncertain, but commonly accepted theories propose embryologic, traumatic, or infectious causes. Osteomata are also found in

sinus, allowing the animal to exhale air through the lower hole of the nasal vestibule. The upper half of the nasal cavity was the main respiratory tract, allowing air to enter via the upper hole of the nasal vestibule. An unusually strongly ossified hard palate was present. The air passages may have allowed the animal to cool the air that it breathed and limit water loss. The hard palate allowed it to eat tough plants. All this suggested that it lived in a hot, arid, environment. There is even some evidence that the animal may have possessed a salt gland

spine. Anterior nasal aperture The anterior nasal aperture (piriform or pyriform aperture) is a heart- or pear-shaped opening in the human skull. Its long axis is vertical, and narrow end upward; in the recent state it is much contracted by the lateral nasal cartilage and the greater and lesser alar cartilages of the nose. It is bounded above by the inferior borders of the nasal bones; laterally by the thin, sharp margins which separate the anterior from the nasal surfaces of the maxilla; and below by the same borders, where they curve medialward to join each other at the anterior

osteoderms of the snoutΓÇöcompletely roofed the enlarged opening of the nostrils, giving a bulbous appearance. The nostrils also had an intranarial septum, which separated the nasal passage from the sinus. Each side of the snout had five sinuses, four of which expanded into the maxilla bone. The nasal cavities (or chambers) of "Ankylosaurus" were elongated and separated by a septum at the midline, which divided the inside of the snout into two mirrored halves. The septum had two openings, including the choanae (internal nostrils). The maxillae expanded to the sides, giving the impression of a bulge, which may have been

Rhinolith A rhinolith is a calculus present in the nasal cavity. The word is derived from the roots "rhino-" and "-lith", literally meaning "nose stone". It is an uncommon medical phenomenon, not to be confused with dried nasal mucus. A rhinolith usually forms around the nucleus of a small exogenous foreign body, blood clot or secretion by slow deposition of calcium and magnesium salts. Over a period of time, they grow into large irregular masses that fill the nasal cavity. They may cause pressure necrosis of the nasal septum or lateral wall of nose. Rhinoliths can cause nasal obstruction, epistaxis,  Answer:

expected_response.answer: The bony structure of the nasal cavity is formed by several bones working together. [d2] An ossified hard palate also contributes to the bony framework associated with the nasal region, forming its floor. [d4] Additionally, a bony nasal septum divides the nasal cavity at the midline into two mirrored halves, with the maxillae expanding to the sides, further illustrating how multiple bones collectively form the nasal cavity's bony structure.

expected_response.answer: The rule of thirds is a deliberate compositional guideline, not a post-hoc rationalization as the query suggests. [d3] and [d4] explain that the basic principle involves dividing an image into thirds both horizontally and vertically to create 9 parts, with the grid identifying four intersection areas where points of interest should be placed to achieve a more balanced image.
